content.content:
1→import {
     2→  BackDropBottom,
     3→  Box,
     4→  Button,
     5→  CardActivity,
     6→  DetailTransactionValue,
     7→  SeparatorHorizontal,
     8→  TagLabel,
     9→  Text,
    10→} from '@semparar/design-system-app';
    11→import { PropsModalizeRef } from '@semparar/design-system-app/src/@types/modelize';
    12→import React, { FC } from 'react';
    13→import { Linking } from 'react-native';
    14→import { eventSelectContent } from '~/constants/functions';
    15→import BasicScreen from '../../atomic/templates/BasicScreen';
    16→import { moment } from '../../services/moment';
    17→import { formatToCurrency } from '../../utils/functions';
    18→import { PointsDetailProps } from './Models';
    19→import {
    20→  getDetailScreenTitle,
    21→  getFormattedHighlightText,
    22→  getFormattedPointsValue,
    23→  getOrigin,
    24→  getPurchaseTypeLabel,
    25→  getTagSubType,
    26→  getTransactionGainType,
    27→  getVehicleInfo,
    28→} from './utils';
    29→
    30→const PointsDetailView: FC<PointsDetailProps> = ({
    31→  handleGoBack,
    32→  transaction,
    33→  modalizeRef,
    34→  handleOpenReportProblem,
    35→  handleCloseReportProblem,
    36→}) => {
    37→  const gainType = getTransactionGainType(transaction);
    38→  const tagSubType = getTagSubType(transaction);
    39→  const screenTitle = getDetailScreenTitle(gainType, transaction.type);
    40→  const vehicleInfo = getVehicleInfo(transaction);
    41→  const purchaseTypeLabel = getPurchaseTypeLabel(tagSubType);
    42→  const origin = getOrigin(transaction);
    43→  const formattedCurrencyValue = formatToCurrency(transaction.currencyValue);
    44→  const formattedPointsValue = getFormattedPointsValue(
    45→    transaction.amount,
    46→    transaction.type,
    47→  );
    48→  const cardTitle = transaction.description || '';
    49→  const highlightText = getFormattedHighlightText(
    50→    transaction.date,
    51→    gainType,
    52→    moment,
    53→  );
    54→
    55→  const renderDivider = () => {
    56→    return (
    57→      <SeparatorHorizontal
    58→        borderWidth="smaller"
    59→        borderColor="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_02"
    60→        height={1}
    61→        width="100%"
    62→        mt="xxxxs"
    63→      />
    64→    );
    65→  };
    66→
    67→  const renderTagContent = () => {
    68→    const hasVehicleInfo = !!vehicleInfo && vehicleInfo !== '';
    69→    const shouldShowOrigin = origin && !hasVehicleInfo;
    70→    const hasPurchaseTypeLabel =
    71→      !!purchaseTypeLabel && purchaseTypeLabel !== '';
    72→
    73→    return (
    74→      <>
    75→        {renderDivider()}
    76→        {shouldShowOrigin && (
    77→          <>
    78→            <Text
    79→              type="paragraphXL"
    80→              color="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_01"
    81→              mb="xxxxs"
    82→              mt="xxxxs"
    83→            >
    84→              Origem
    85→            </Text>
    86→            <Text type="paragraphMD" color="INTENSE_COLOR_03">
    87→              {origin}
    88→            </Text>
    89→            {renderDivider()}
    90→          </>
    91→        )}
    92→        {hasVehicleInfo && (
    93→          <>
    94→            <Text
    95→              type="paragraphXL"
    96→              color="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_01"
    97→              mb="xxxxs"
    98→              mt="xxxxs"
    99→            >
   100→              Tag
   101→            </Text>
   102→            <Text type="paragraphMD" color="INTENSE_COLOR_03">
   103→              {vehicleInfo}
   104→            </Text>
   105→            {renderDivider()}
   106→          </>
   107→        )}
   108→
   109→        {hasPurchaseTypeLabel && (
   110→          <>
   111→            <Text
   112→              type="paragraphXL"
   113→              color="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_01"
   114→              mb="xxxxs"
   115→              mt="xxxxs"
   116→            >
   117→              Tipo de compra
   118→            </Text>
   119→            <Text type="paragraphMD" color="INTENSE_COLOR_03">
   120→              {purchaseTypeLabel}
   121→            </Text>
   122→          </>
   123→        )}
   124→      </>
   125→    );
   126→  };
   127→
   128→  return (
   129→    <>
   130→      <BasicScreen
   131→        title={screenTitle}
   132→        leftIconOnPress={handleGoBack}
   133→        hasScroll
   134→        errorButtonOnPress={() => {}}
   135→      >
   136→        <DetailTransactionValue
   137→          icon={transaction.icon as never}
   138→          title={cardTitle}
   139→          titleColor="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_01"
   140→          iconBgColor="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_03"
   141→          points={formattedPointsValue}
   142→          bgColor="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_02"
   143→          pointsColor={
   144→            transaction.type === 'CREDIT' || transaction.type === 'REFUNDED'
   145→              ? 'PLUS_COLOR_02'
   146→              : ('INTENSE_COLOR_05' as const)
   147→          }
   148→        />
   149→
   150→        <Box ml="smaller" mr="smaller" mt="xxxxs" flex={1}>
   151→          <TagLabel
   152→            icon={undefined}
   153→            type="neutral"
   154→            text={highlightText}
   155→            showSpacing
   156→          />
   157→
   158→          <Box mt="xxxs" flex={1} width="100%">
   159→            <Text type="paragraphXL" color="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_01" mb="xxxxs">
   160→              Valor
   161→            </Text>
   162→            <Text type="paragraphMD" color="INTENSE_COLOR_03">
   163→              {formattedCurrencyValue}
   164→            </Text>
   165→
   166→            {gainType === 'TAG' && renderTagContent()}
   167→          </Box>
   168→
   169→          <Box width="100%" align="flex-end" mb="small">
   170→            <Button
   171→              type="secundaryLarge"
   172→              text="Relatar Problema"
   173→              onPress={handleOpenReportProblem}
   174→            />
   175→          </Box>
   176→        </Box>
   177→      </BasicScreen>
   178→
   179→      <BackDropBottom modalizeRef={modalizeRef as PropsModalizeRef}>
   180→        <Box align="center" width="100%" mt="xxs">
   181→          <Box align="center" width="100%">
   182→            <Text
   183→              type="paragraphXXXL"
   184→              color="BRAND_COMPLEMENT_01"
   185→              mb="xs"
   186→              align="center"
   187→            >
   188→              Central de Atendimento
   189→            </Text>
   190→          </Box>
   191→        </Box>
   192→
   193→        <Box pd="innerSmall">
   194→          <CardActivity
   195→            mt="nano"
   196→            icon="icon-contact"
   197→            title="Capitais"
   198→            description="4002 1552"
   199→            iconRight="icon-next"
   200→            showIcon
   201→            showTagLabel={false}
   202→            onPress={() => {
   203→              eventSelectContent({
   204→                content_type: 'cta',
   205→                content_id: 'telefones_capitais',
   206→                location_1: 'monetizacao',
   207→                location_2: 'hub_recompensas_spmais',
   208→                location_3: 'contato_telefones',
   209→              });
   210→              Linking.openURL('tel:40021552');
   211→            }}
   212→          />
   213→
   214→          <CardActivity
   215→            mt="nano"
   216→            icon="icon-contact"
   217→            title="Demais localidades"
   218→            description="0800 015 0252"
   219→            iconRight="icon-next"
   220→            showIcon
   221→            showTagLabel={false}
   222→            onPress={() => {
   223→              eventSelectContent({
   224→                content_type: 'cta',
   225→                content_id: 'telefones_demais_localidades',
   226→                location_1: 'monetizacao',
   227→                location_2: 'hub_recompensas_spmais',
   228→                location_3: 'contato_telefones',
   229→              });
   230→

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Storybook 8 addon panel right-side sidebar layout configuration:

## Configuration for Right-Side Panel Layout

When viewing a story, Storybook displays the addon panel on the bottom or right of the UI, showing UIs for available addons (e.g., the interactions panel, accessibility tests panel or controls panel).

To position the addon panel on the right side by default in Storybook 8, you can configure the `panelPosition` setting in your `.storybook/manager.js` file:

You can set `panelPosition` to "right" in a `.storybook/manager.js` file with documentation on how to set this up.

## Configuration Example

The configuration includes options like `navSize`, `bottomPanelHeight`, `rightPanelWidth`, and `panelPosition` set to 'bottom', which you can modify in your manager configuration.

For a right-side layout, you would set `panelPosition: 'right'` along with `rightPanelWidth` to control the width of the right panel in your `addons.setConfig()` call within the manager.js file.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Storybook 8 addon panel right-side sidebar layout configuration:

## Configuration for Right-Side Panel Layout

When viewing a story, Storybook displays the addon panel on the bottom or right of the UI, showing UIs for available addons (e.g., the interactions panel, accessibility tests panel or controls panel).

To position the addon panel on the right side by default in Storybook 8, you can configure the `panelPosition` setting in your `.storybook/manager.js` file:

You can set `panelPosition` to "right" in a `.storybook/manager.js` file with documentation on how to set this up.

## Configuration Example

The configuration includes options like `navSize`, `bottomPanelHeight`, `rightPanelWidth`, and `panelPosition` set to 'bottom', which you can modify in your manager configuration.

For a right-side layout, you would set `panelPosition: 'right'` along with `rightPanelWidth` to control the width of the right panel in your `addons.setConfig()` call within the manager.js file.

input.prompt: How to position the addon panel (Controls) to the right by default in Storybook 8? What is the solution/code?

content.content:
# Positioning the Controls Panel to the Right in Storybook

To position the addon panel (Controls) to the right by default in Storybook, you need to configure the `panelPosition` setting.

## Solution

Create or edit a `.storybook/manager.js` file and set `panelPosition` to `"right"`:

```javascript
addons.setConfig({
  panelPosition: 'right'
});
```

This configuration will persist the panel position to the right side of the canvas, preventing it from resetting to the bottom on page reload.

## Reference

The official Storybook documentation on "Features and Behavior" provides detailed guidance on implementing this and other manager-level configurations: https://storybook.js.org/docs/angular/configure/features-and-behavior

This approach works across Storybook versions including version 8, as the `panelPosition` configuration remains a standard feature-behavior setting.

## Solution

Create or edit a `.storybook/manager.js` file and set `panelPosition` to `"right"`:

```javascript
addons.setConfig({
  panelPosition: 'right'
});
```

This configuration will persist the panel position to the right side of the canvas, preventing it from resetting to the bottom on page reload.

## Reference

The official Storybook documentation on "Features and Behavior" provides detailed guidance on implementing this and other manager-level configurations: https://storybook.js.org/docs/angular/configure/features-and-behavior

This approach works across Storybook versions including version 8, as the `panelPosition` configuration remains a standard feature-behavior setting.

input.prompt: What are all the available options for addons.setConfig() in manager.js? Specifically: panelPosition, showPanel, selectedPanel, rightPanelWidth, and any other panel-related options.

content.content:
# Available Panel-Related Options in addons.setConfig()

Based on the Storybook documentation, here are the key panel configuration options:

## Core Panel Options

**panelPosition**: Controls where the addon panel appears. Accepts `'bottom'` or `'right'` as values, determining whether it displays below or beside the preview canvas.

**rightPanelWidth**: Sets the pixel width of the addon panel when positioned on the right side of the interface.

**bottomPanelHeight**: Defines the pixel height when the panel is in the bottom position.

**selectedPanel**: Specifies which addon panel should be displayed by default using its ID (e.g., `'storybook/actions/panel'`).

## Advanced Panel Control

The documentation also mentions `showPanel` as a function within `layoutCustomisations`. This allows conditional visibility based on story state:

> "function...enable you to include some default behavior and can be overridden to customize the UI to your needs"

This function receives parameters like `viewMode`, `storyId`, `tags`, and the current `layout` state, letting you hide panels on specific stories or documentation pages.

## Related Configuration

**showToolbar** and **showSidebar** work similarly, offering dynamic control over other UI sections based on the same state parameters.

These options provide granular control over Storybook's interface layout and panel visibility behavior.

Review this small change as a pull request. The task is to make the Storybook JSON Editor addon panel appear on the right side by default (instead of the bottom).

File created: /Users/anotonio.silva/dev/rnm-app-fintech-rewardprogram/.storybook/manager.ts

Context:
- Storybook 8.6.17 with @storybook/react-native-web-vite
- JSON Editor addon registered in .storybook/addons/json-editor/register.tsx as type PANEL with ID 'sdui-json-editor/panel'
- This manager.ts sets panelPosition to 'right', rightPanelWidth to 400, and selectedPanel to the JSON Editor
- Screenshot validated: panel now appears on right side

Check: correctness, import path, potential issues. Be concise.

content.text:
Perfect. Here's my PR review:

---

## Review: manager.ts - JSON Editor Panel Position Configuration

**Status: APPROVED**

### Correctness ✓

**Import path**: Correct. `storybook/internal/manager-api` is the standard Storybook 8+ manager API import.

**Panel ID reference**: Valid. The `'sdui-json-editor/panel'` ID matches exactly the `PANEL_ID` from `.storybook/addons/json-editor/constants.ts`, which constructs it as `${ADDON_ID}/panel` where `ADDON_ID = 'sdui-json-editor'`.

**Configuration object**:
- `panelPosition: 'right'` — Standard Storybook option; places the panel on the right sidebar.
- `rightPanelWidth: 400` — Reasonable default (400px); wide enough for JSON editor without overwhelming the story canvas.
- `selectedPanel: 'sdui-json-editor/panel'` — Ensures the JSON Editor tab is active on load.

### Execution Flow ✓

1. Manager is loaded before any stories render.
2. `addons.setConfig()` applies settings globally before panels initialize.
3. Panel is already registered in `main.ts` → `addons/json-editor/register.tsx`, so the ID will resolve correctly.

### No Issues Detected

- No circular dependencies.
- No missing dependencies.
- No conflicts with Storybook essentials or other addons.
- Screenshot validates correct UX result.

**Recommendation**: Merge. This is a minimal, clean, and correct configuration change.

  > Created .claude/settings.json with @playwright/mcp MCP server (headless).
  > Requires Claude Code session restart to activate MCP tools.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0001.0 — Setup Storybook
- [DONE] Configure Storybook so this MFE can render screens independently of the
SuperApp.
  > Storybook 8.6.17 with @storybook/react-native-web-vite running on port 6006.
  > Custom Vite plugins: jsxInJsPlugin, dtsStubPlugin, circularFixPlugin, cjsToEsmPlugin.
  > ThemeProvider decorator in preview.tsx. Zustand store mocked in .storybook/mocks/store.ts.
  > SmokeTest story renders Text, Button, Box from DS successfully.

Context:
- This is a React Native MFE (package.json: react 18.2.0, react-native 0.73.6)
- The design system (@semparar/design-system-app ^3.39.15) already has Storybook
  configured — its setup can be used as reference if the executing agent needs it
- Babel aliases: ~ → ./src/ (babel.config.js line 27)
- TS alias: ~/store → ./@types/store.d.ts (tsconfig.json line 11) — this is a
  module augmentation for the SuperApp's Zustand store, needs mocking
- DS components depend on design tokens — may need a ThemeProvider or similar

Deliverable: `npm run storybook` opens a browser with a smoke test story that
renders at least one DS component (e.g., Button or Text from @semparar/design-system-app).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0001.1 — Validate DS components in Storybook
- [DONE] Prove that the design system components actually used in voucher screens
      render correctly in the Storybook web context.
  > 8/10 DS components render in Storybook web context.
  > BackDropBottom (Modalize) and Loading (Lottie) require native modules — cannot render on web.
  > SDUI adapters should provide web-safe fallbacks for these two.
  > Full validation story at src/__stories__/DSComponentValidation.stories.tsx.

Context:
- The voucher screens use these DS components: Text, Button, Box, InputBox,
  Alert, Icon, ListTextIcon, SeparatorHorizontal, BackDropBottom, Loading
- See existing usage in:
  - src/screens/InvoiceCashbackPoints/view.tsx (form with inputs, balance, button)
  - src/screens/InvoiceCashbackSummary/view.tsx (review, success, error states)
  - src/screens/InvoiceCashbackOnboarding/index.tsx (list items with icons)
- Some components may not render in web — document which ones work and which don't

Deliverable: A story page rendering each DS component listed above. Annotate any
that fail or need workarounds.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0002.0 — SDUI Core: Types & Interfaces
- [DONE] Define the TypeScript type system for the SDUI architecture described in
      .claude/rfc-conteudo-dinamico.txt (sections 3.1–3.5).
  > Types at src/services/sdui/models/index.ts (180 lines).
  > Covers: SDUIScreen, SDUIComponent, SDUIContext, SDUIRichText, SDUIRule,
  >   SDUIConditional, SDUIValidation, SDUIComponentRegistry, SDUIComponentRenderer.
  > SDUI_KNOWN_TYPES const array with all 13 voucher-relevant component types.

Context:
- Screen structure: { id, context, header, body[], footer } (RFC section 3.1)
- Components: { type, props } (RFC section 3.2)
- Rich text: { type: "rich_text", children: [nodes] } (RFC section 3.3)
- Context interpolation: {{var}} pattern (RFC section 3.4)
- Conditionals: JSON Logic subset (RFC section 3.5)
- Follow project conventions: .claude/STYLE_GUIDE.md (services architecture,
  models in Models/ folders)

Deliverable: Type definitions in src/services/sdui/models/ that contract the
entire SDUI system (Screen, Component, Context, RichText, ComponentRegistry).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0002.1 — SDUI Core: Interpolation Parser
- [DONE] Implement the {{var}} replacement engine from RFC section 3.4.
  > src/services/sdui/utils/interpolation.ts — 3 functions:
  >   resolveContextPath (nested path + array index support)
  >   interpolateString ({{var}} regex replacement)
  >   interpolateDeep (recursive object/array walk)
  > 24 unit tests passing (interpolation.test.ts).
  > Storybook demo at src/__stories__/SDUIInterpolation.stories.tsx.
  > Screenshot validated via Playwright.

Context:
- Must resolve nested paths: {{product.name}}, {{balance.pointsFormatted}}
- Must deep-walk an object interpolating all string values in props
- Missing values should resolve gracefully (empty string, not crash)
- Existing interpolation example in RFC:
  "Voucher para {{product.name}}" + context.product.name = "Abastecimento"
  → "Voucher para Abastecimento"
- Types from SP_0002.0 should be used

Deliverable: Interpolation utility with unit tests.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0002.2 — SDUI Core: Component Registry & Screen Renderer
- [DONE] Build the rendering engine that turns a JSON screen definition into React
      components. This is the core of the SDUI architecture (RFC sections 3.1–3.2).
  > Registry at src/services/sdui/registry/index.ts — createRegistry, registerComponent,
  >   getRenderer, registerToDefault (immutable API + mutable default singleton).
  > ScreenRenderer at src/services/sdui/renderer/ScreenRenderer.tsx — renders header,
  >   body[], footer from SDUIScreen JSON. Applies interpolateDeep to all props.
  > FallbackComponent at src/services/sdui/renderer/FallbackComponent.tsx — visible
  >   yellow box for unknown component types (uses RN primitives for ThemeProvider safety).
  > 14 unit tests passing (registry.test.ts + ScreenRenderer.test.tsx).
  > Storybook demo at src/__stories__/SDUIScreenRenderer.stories.tsx.
  > Screenshot validated via Playwright.

Context:
- Registry maps string type → React component (RFC section 3.2 "Componentes")
- Renderer receives SDUIScreen JSON, iterates body[], renders each via registry
- Must apply interpolation (SP_0002.1) to all string props using screen context
- Unknown types should render a visible fallback (not crash)
- Types from SP_0002.0, interpolation from SP_0002.1

Deliverable: A <ScreenRenderer screen={json} /> component that renders a
screen definition from JSON.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0002.3 — SDUI Component Adapters
- [DONE] Create adapters that bridge SDUI component types to actual DS components.
  > 11 adapters implemented: heading, text, section_heading, balance_display,
  >   points_input, button, link, alert, list_text_icon, separator, icon_text_row.
  > All registered via registerAllAdapters() in src/services/sdui/adapters/index.ts.
  > 13 unit tests passing (adapters.test.tsx).
  > Storybook story at src/__stories__/SDUIAdapters.stories.tsx.
  > Screenshots validated: sdui-adapters.png + sdui-adapters-full.png.
  > Review notes: stateful PointsInputAdapter + stubbed onPress handlers are
  >   acceptable tech debt for spike phase.

Context:
- Each adapter maps SDUI props to @semparar/design-system-app component props
- Required adapters based on voucher screen analysis (TASK.pdf + existing screens):

  | SDUI type        | DS component(s)           | Used in                    |
  |------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
  | heading          | Text (headingMDL)         | Form title, Review title   |
  | text             | Text (configurable)       | Labels, descriptions       |
  | balance_display  | Box + Text                | "Pontos disponíveis: X"   |
  | points_input     | InputBox                  | Points/reais fields        |
  | button           | Button                    | CTA buttons                |
  | link             | Text (pressable)          | "Exibir regras de uso"     |
  | alert            | Alert                     | Info boxes in summary      |
  | list_text_icon   | ListTextIcon              | Onboarding items, rules    |
  | separator        | SeparatorHorizontal       | Between fields             |
  | icon_text_row    | Box + Icon + Text         | Regras de uso bullet items |
  | section_heading  | Text (subtitleXXL)        | Regras section titles      |

- Register all in the default registry from SP_0002.2
- DS component validation from SP_0001.1 may affect which adapters are viable

Deliverable: All voucher-relevant adapters registered in the component registry.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0002.4 — Mock JSON Payloads
- [DONE] Create realistic JSON payloads for the voucher screens described in TASK.pdf.
  > 8 payloads: 4 screens (form, summary, conclusion, rules) × 2 products (Abastecimento 206, Estacionamento 205).
  > Files at src/services/sdui/mocks/ — voucherForm.ts, voucherSummary.ts, voucherConclusion.ts, voucherRules.ts.
  > Barrel export at src/services/sdui/mocks/index.ts.
  > All payloads use {{var}} interpolation for dynamic content (balance, conversion, product name).
  > Storybook validation story at src/__stories__/SDUIMockPayloads.stories.tsx.
  > Screenshot validated via Playwright (sdui-mockpayloads-full.png).
  > Review notes: icon_text_row with empty text in conclusion screen is a known spike limitation
  >   (would need a new `status_icon` type for proper centered icon rendering).

Context:
- 4 screens need payloads (see TASK.pdf pages 2–4 for visual reference):
  1. Voucher form (points/reais input) — reference: InvoiceCashbackPoints/view.tsx
  2. Voucher summary (review before confirm) — reference: InvoiceCashbackSummary/view.tsx lines 47-151
  3. Voucher conclusion (success) — reference: InvoiceCashbackSummary/view.tsx lines 154-203
  4. Regras de uso (rules) — reference: TASK.pdf page 4 screenshot
- Product codes (src/screens/Points/data/index.ts lines 62-70):
  206 = Abastecimento, 205 = Estacionamento, 207 = Seguro Auto, 208 = IPVA
- Text differences per product visible in TASK.pdf page 2:
  - Cashback: "Defina quanto cashback quer na sua fatura"
  - Voucher: "Defina o desconto para seu abastecimento"
  - Cashback: "Cashback aplicado" / Voucher: "O voucher foi criado"
  - Cashback: "Confirmar desconto" / Voucher: "Criar voucher"
- Static content (labels, titles, rules) goes in JSON. Dynamic data (balance,
  points) comes from context — not hardcoded in the payload.
- Include at least 2 product variants (Abastecimento + Estacionamento) to prove
  the same renderer handles different products via different JSON.

Deliverable: JSON payloads for 4 screens × 2 products.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0002.5 — Demo Stories: SDUI Rendering
- [DONE] Final demonstration — render all voucher screens from JSON through the SDUI
      engine in Storybook.
  > 5 stories in src/__stories__/SDUIDemoVoucher.stories.tsx:
  >   Form, Summary, Conclusion, Rules (individual with product toggle)
  >   FullFlow (combined: product toggle + step navigation across all 4 screens).
  > Product toggle proves: same ScreenRenderer + adapters, different JSON = different screens.
  > nav_header shows as yellow fallback (expected — no DS adapter for navigation).
  > Screenshots validated via Playwright (sdui-demo-form, summary, conclusion, rules, fullflow).
  > Review: no blocking issues. Hard-coded colors in toggle buttons acceptable for spike.

Context:
- Uses: ScreenRenderer (SP_0002.2), adapters (SP_0002.3), payloads (SP_0002.4)
- Each story feeds a mock JSON payload into <ScreenRenderer />
- Key demonstration: switching product context (Abastecimento → Estacionamento)
  changes the rendered screen WITHOUT changing any code — only JSON differs
- This is the spike's central proof: same code, different JSON, different screens

Deliverable: Storybook stories showing all 4 voucher screens rendered from JSON,
with product variant toggle proving reusability. This is the spike's final output.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_XGH986 Feature Planning & Ideation
- [DONE] Brainstorming & task creation based on ideation frameworks.
  > Applied SCAMPER (Substitute/Combine/Adapt/Eliminate) + Jobs-to-be-Done
  > to identify DX improvements for the rewards program MFE.
  > Core insight: "developers need to validate UI changes without deploying
  > to the SuperApp" → Storybook as the primary inner-loop dev tool.
  > Tasks SP_0003.x (foundation), SP_0004.x (screens), SP_0005.x (SDUI expansion)
  > created below.
  >
  > Ideation frameworks used:
  > - SCAMPER: What can we Substitute (SuperApp → Storybook), Combine
  >   (JSON editor + live preview), Adapt (SDUI to existing screens),
  >   Eliminate (API/login dependencies)?
  > - JTBD: "When I change a screen, I want to see the result immediately
  >   without navigating through the SuperApp login/navigation flow"
  > - DX Inner Loop: Optimize write→see→validate cycle from minutes to seconds
  > - Apple Creative Selection (Ken Kocienda): Demo-driven development —
  >   build it, see it, iterate. Storybook IS the demo environment.
  >
  > References:
  > - SCAMPER: https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/learn-how-to-use-the-best-ideation-methods-scamper
  > - DX Framework: https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.06352
  > - DevEx (ACM): https://queue.acm.org/detail.cfm?id=3595878
  > - Creative Selection (book): https://creativeselection.io/

================================================================================
=== PHASE 3: STORYBOOK FOUNDATION IMPROVEMENTS ================================
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0003.0 — Fix Storybook Fonts to Match Native App
- [DONE] Configure Storybook web fonts to match the native app typography.
  > Created .storybook/preview-head.html with @font-face declarations for Ubuntu
  >   (Light/Regular/Medium/Bold + Italics) + RN Web aliases (Ubuntu-Light, Ubuntu-Medium, etc.)
  >   + icon fonts (IconsSemParar, icomoon). All with font-display: swap.
  > Added staticDirs to .storybook/main.ts serving fonts from DS package + local src/assets/fonts.
  > Global font-family scoped to #storybook-root (avoids breaking Storybook UI and icon fonts).
  > Screenshots validated: fonts-final-fullflow.png, fonts-final-adapters.png.

Context:
- The native app uses custom fonts (likely from @semparar/design-system-app tokens)
- Storybook web renders with system fallback fonts, causing visual mismatch
- Check DS theme tokens for font family definitions:
  - Look at ThemeProvider in .storybook/preview.tsx
  - Check @semparar/design-system-app for fontFamily tokens
  - Look at the native app's font assets (likely in assets/fonts/ or similar)
- Solution likely involves:
  a. Finding the .ttf/.otf font files used by the native app
  b. Adding @font-face declarations in .storybook/preview-head.html or a CSS file
  c. Ensuring DS theme tokens reference the correct web font-family names
- Compare: Figma tiles (.claude/tiles/figma-*.png) vs current Storybook screenshots
  (.storybook/screenshots/sdui-adapters-full.png) to validate visual parity

Deliverable: Storybook renders text with the same font family as the native app.
Screenshot comparison proving font match.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0003.1 — Extract and Index Figma PDF Pages
- [DONE] Extract all 456 pages of .claude/full-design.pdf as individual PNG tiles
      and create a page index documenting which screens are on which pages.
  > All 456 pages extracted as .claude/tiles/figma-{001..456}.png at 150 DPI.
  > Page index created at .claude/figma-index.md with:
  >   - ~280 SCREEN pages (>20KB), ~55 SMALL pages (5-20KB), ~121 BLANK pages (<5KB)
  >   - Pages 121-456 visually scanned by AI vision agents with content descriptions
  >   - Pages 1-120 categorized by size; key screens identified from task docs
  >   - Key screen groups: Voucher List/Details, Cashback/Invoice, Voucher Creation
  >     (Parking, IPVA/Debts, Auto Insurance), Transfer/Pix, Extrato
  >   - Consistent UX pattern identified: Onboarding → Form → Confirmation → Success + Rules
  > Note: pages 1-120 marked "(unscanned)" — haiku agents hit image dimension limits
  >   for batch processing. Descriptions available for known reference pages only.

Deliverable: All 456 pages extracted as .claude/tiles/figma-*.png +
.claude/figma-index.md mapping pages to screen names.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0003.2 — Storybook JSON Editor Addon (IndexedDB Persistence)
- [DONE] Create a custom Storybook addon panel that allows editing the SDUI JSON
      payload in real-time, with automatic persistence to IndexedDB.
  > 6 files in .storybook/addons/json-editor/:
  >   register.tsx (addon registration), JsonEditorPanel.tsx (editor UI),
  >   useSDUIJsonEditor.ts (preview-side hook), useIndexedDB.ts (persistence),
  >   constants.ts (shared IDs/events), index.ts (barrel exports).
  > Features: textarea with monospace font, Format/Reset buttons, debounced
  >   auto-save to IndexedDB keyed by story ID, JSON validation with inline errors,
  >   "edited" badge when payload differs from default, live preview via channel events.
  > Integrated in main.ts addons array + used by SDUIDemoVoucher stories.
  > Review fixes applied: IDB error handling (.catch), db.close() on cleanup,
  >   debounce timer cleanup on unmount.
  > Screenshot validated: json-editor-validated.png.

Context:
- Every SDUI story should have a companion JSON editor panel
- The editor should:
  a. Show the current SDUIScreen JSON in a textarea or code editor
  b. Parse and validate JSON on change (show errors inline)
  c. Apply changes live to the ScreenRenderer preview
  d. Auto-save to IndexedDB keyed by story ID so edits persist across refreshes
  e. Have a "Reset to Default" button to restore the original mock payload
- Implementation approach:
  a. Create .storybook/addons/json-editor/ with:
     - register.tsx (Storybook addon registration)
     - JsonEditorPanel.tsx (the editor UI component)
     - useIndexedDB.ts (IndexedDB read/write hook)
  b. Register as a panel addon in .storybook/main.ts
  c. Use Storybook's addons API: addons.register(), addons.addPanel()
  d. Communicate with stories via Storybook channel events
- Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/addons/writing-addons
- The editor should work with ALL SDUI stories (current and future)
- Consider using a simple <textarea> with monospace font for MVP — no need for
  a full Monaco editor initially
- IndexedDB key format: `sdui-json-${storyId}`

Deliverable: A "JSON Editor" panel tab in Storybook that edits SDUI payloads
live with IndexedDB persistence. Works with all SDUI stories.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0003.3 — Storybook Phone Frame Decorator
- [DONE] Create a decorator that wraps stories in a phone-shaped frame to better
      simulate the native app viewport.
  > Created .storybook/decorators/PhoneFrameDecorator.tsx with:
  >   - Phone bezel (dark border, rounded corners, notch, home indicator)
  >   - 3 device presets: iPhone 14 (390×844), iPhone 14 Pro Max (430×932), Pixel 7 (412×915)
  >   - Toolbar toggle: "Frame On/Off" button + device selector dropdown
  >   - Content area with overflow: auto, word-break: break-word for long text safety
  > Integrated in .storybook/preview.tsx (decorators + globalTypes + initialGlobals).
  > Screenshots validated: phone-frame-form.png, phone-frame-fullflow.png, phone-frame-summary.png.
  > Also covers SP0XM01 (mobile frame + long text fix).

Context:
- Current stories render at full browser width, which doesn't represent the
  actual mobile experience
- A phone frame decorator would:
  a. Wrap the story in a container with fixed width (~375px) and aspect ratio

================================================================================
=== PHASE 6: RFC MISSING IMPLEMENTATIONS ======================================
================================================================================

NOTE: These tasks implement RFC features that have types defined (SP_0002.0)
but no runtime code. They complete the SDUI engine to full RFC spec.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.0 — JSON Logic Evaluator
- [ ] Implement the JSON Logic rule evaluator described in RFC section 3.5.

Context:
- RFC defines operators: var (data access), ==, !=, >, <, >=, <= (comparison),
  and, or, ! (logical)
- Types already exist: SDUIRule, SDUIComparisonRule, SDUILogicalRule,
  SDUIVarOperator, SDUIConditional (in src/services/sdui/models/index.ts)
- Needs a function: evaluateRule(rule: SDUIRule, context: SDUIContext): boolean
- var operator uses resolveContextPath from interpolation.ts
- Invalid/malformed rules should return false (RFC 6.4: "Regras inválidas
  retornam false")
- For `visible` conditionals specifically: invalid rules should default to
  true (show component) — hiding content by default on bad data is worse UX
- Must be pure function, no side effects
- Reference: https://jsonlogic.com/

Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/jsonLogic.ts with evaluateRule() +
comprehensive unit tests covering all operators and edge cases.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.1 — Component Visibility & Button Enabled Conditionals
- [ ] Wire the JSON Logic evaluator into ScreenRenderer for component
      visibility and button enabled state.

Context:
- SDUIComponent already has optional `visible: SDUIConditional` field
- ScreenRenderer currently ignores `visible` — should skip rendering when
  evaluateRule returns false
- ButtonAdapter should process `enabled: { rule: SDUIRule }` in props —
  disable button when rule evaluates to false
- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
- Update existing ScreenRenderer.test.tsx and adapters.test.tsx
- Add Storybook demo showing conditional visibility toggle

Deliverable: Components with `visible` conditionals are hidden/shown based on
context. Buttons with `enabled` rules are disabled/enabled dynamically.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.2 — Input Validation Rules
- [ ] Implement validation rule execution for SDUI input components.

Context:
- RFC section 3.5 defines validation pattern:
  { "validation": { "rules": [{ "rule": {...}, "message": "error text" }] } }
- Types exist: SDUIValidationRule, SDUIValidation
- PointsInputAdapter has textError prop but no validation integration
- Needs: validateField(value, validation: SDUIValidation, context): string | null
  (returns first failing rule's message, or null if valid)
- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
- Should integrate with PointsInputAdapter's error display
- Scope: validation utility function only. Form-level orchestration (when to
  trigger: blur/change/submit) is a separate concern for action handlers
- Add Storybook demo showing validation in action

Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/validation.ts with validateField() +
integration into PointsInputAdapter + unit tests.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.3 — Rich Text Adapter
- [ ] Implement the rich_text component adapter described in RFC section 3.3.

Context:
- RFC defines rich text as array of typed nodes:
  - text: { type: "text", value: string, bold?: boolean, italic?: boolean, color?: string }
  - link: { type: "link", text: string, to: string, trackEvent?: string }
  - icon: { type: "icon", name: string, color?: string, size?: number }
  - linebreak: { type: "linebreak" }
- Types already defined: SDUIRichText, SDUITextNode, SDUILinkNode, SDUIIconNode,
  SDUILineBreakNode (in models/index.ts)
- Should render inline — all nodes in a single wrapping Text component
  (React Native supports nested Text for inline formatting)
- Links should use DS Text with underline style (similar to LinkAdapter)
- Icons use DS Icon component inline
- Linebreak nodes render as `\n` inside Text (not DOM br element —
  React Native Text supports newline characters)
- Register as "rich_text" type in adapter registry
- Add mock payload using rich_text to test inline formatting

Deliverable: RichTextAdapter registered in SDUI registry + unit tests +
Storybook demo showing mixed bold/link/icon inline text.

   739→- Needs a function: evaluateRule(rule: SDUIRule, context: SDUIContext): boolean
   740→- var operator uses resolveContextPath from interpolation.ts
   741→- Invalid/malformed rules should return false (RFC 6.4: "Regras inválidas
   742→  retornam false")
   743→- For `visible` conditionals specifically: invalid rules should default to
   744→  true (show component) — hiding content by default on bad data is worse UX
   745→- Must be pure function, no side effects
   746→- Reference: https://jsonlogic.com/
   747→
   748→Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/jsonLogic.ts with evaluateRule() +
   749→comprehensive unit tests covering all operators and edge cases.
   750→
   751→--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   752→
   753→# SP_0006.1 — Component Visibility & Button Enabled Conditionals
   754→- [ ] Wire the JSON Logic evaluator into ScreenRenderer for component
   755→      visibility and button enabled state.
   756→
   757→Context:
   758→- SDUIComponent already has optional `visible: SDUIConditional` field
   759→- ScreenRenderer currently ignores `visible` — should skip rendering when
   760→  evaluateRule returns false
   761→- ButtonAdapter should process `enabled: { rule: SDUIRule }` in props —
   762→  disable button when rule evaluates to false
   763→- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
   764→- Update existing ScreenRenderer.test.tsx and adapters.test.tsx
   765→- Add Storybook demo showing conditional visibility toggle
   766→
   767→Deliverable: Components with `visible` conditionals are hidden/shown based on
   768→context. Buttons with `enabled` rules are disabled/enabled dynamically.
   769→
   770→--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   771→
   772→# SP_0006.2 — Input Validation Rules
   773→- [ ] Implement validation rule execution for SDUI input components.
   774→
   775→Context:
   776→- RFC section 3.5 defines validation pattern:
   777→  { "validation": { "rules": [{ "rule": {...}, "message": "error text" }] } }
   778→- Types exist: SDUIValidationRule, SDUIValidation
   779→- PointsInputAdapter has textError prop but no validation integration
   780→- Needs: validateField(value, validation: SDUIValidation, context): string | null
   781→  (returns first failing rule's message, or null if valid)
   782→- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
   783→- Should integrate with PointsInputAdapter's error display
   784→- Scope: validation utility function only. Form-level orchestration (when to
   785→  trigger: blur/change/submit) is a separate concern for action handlers
   786→- Add Storybook demo showing validation in action
   787→
   788→Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/validation.ts with validateField() +
   789→integration into PointsInputAdapter + unit tests.
   790→
   791→--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   792→
   793→# SP_0006.3 — Rich Text Adapter
   794→- [ ] Implement the rich_text component adapter described in RFC section 3.3.
   795→
   796→Context:
   797→- RFC defines rich text as array of typed nodes:
   798→  - text: { type: "text", value: string, bold?: boolean, italic?: boolean, color?: string }
   799→  - link: { type: "link", text: string, to: string, trackEvent?: string }
   800→  - icon: { type: "icon", name: string, color?: string, size?: number }
   801→  - linebreak: { type: "linebreak" }
   802→- Types already defined: SDUIRichText, SDUITextNode, SDUILinkNode, SDUIIconNode,
   803→  SDUILineBreakNode (in models/index.ts)
   804→- Should render inline — all nodes in a single wrapping Text component
   805→  (React Native supports nested Text for inline formatting)
   806→- Links should use DS Text with underline style (similar to LinkAdapter)
   807→- Icons use DS Icon component inline
   808→- Linebreak nodes render as `\n` inside Text (not DOM br element —
   809→  React Native Text supports newline characters)
   810→- Register as "rich_text" type in adapter registry
   811→- Add mock payload using rich_text to test inline formatting
   812→
   813→Deliverable: RichTextAdapter registered in SDUI registry + unit tests +
   814→Storybook demo showing mixed bold/link/icon inline text.
   815→
   816→--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   817→
   818→
   819→# SP008767GX
   820→- [ ] Find all design system component used in our screens and map all of it to
   821→the SDUI system, including stories.
   822→
   823→Context: ...
   824→
   825→--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   826→
   827→# SP9999999999 [P99] Multiple tasks
   828→- [ ] ensure stories are handled by `npm run lint`
   829→- [ ] run `npm run lint` and register new tasks for fixing everything
   830→- [ ] implement the task described in ./TASK.pdf
   831→     - Prepare the frontend
   832→     - Test every screen scenario in storybook
   833→     - Create plan/spec to the backend to implement
   834→- [ ] Read all screens and plan what frontend action handlers we will need to
   835→implement from the frontend side (like link redirections, validations, sum
   836→values, custom logics, etc.) in order to make all screen possible to be recreated
   837→ using the SDUI engine then plan the architecture to handle it then map the
   838→ handlers.
   839→

   846→including API connection (see files inside ./.claude/resources)

================================================================================
=== PHASE 5: SDUI EXPANSION — DEMONSTRATING FUTURE MIGRATION ==================
================================================================================

NOTE: These tasks create STORYBOOK-ONLY demonstrations showing how existing
screens COULD be migrated to SDUI in the future. They are proposals, not
production code. Each demo should show side-by-side: current screen vs
SDUI-rendered equivalent from JSON.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0005.0 — SDUI Demo: Meus Vouchers List as SDUI
- [ ] Create an SDUI JSON payload and new adapters to render the "Meus Vouchers"
      list screen via ScreenRenderer, demonstrating SDUI viability for list screens.

Context:
- Figma reference: .claude/tiles/figma-004.png, figma-010.png
- Needs new SDUI adapter types:
  - voucher_card: renders a card with status badge, title, discount value,
    expandable detail (Desconto total, Utilizado, Restante, etc.)
  - status_badge: renders colored tag (Disponível=green, Usado=gray,
    Expirado=yellow, Usado parcialmente=orange)
  - card_list: container that renders a list of child components
- Create mock JSON payload with sample voucher data
- Story should show: list view, expanded card, empty state — all from JSON
- This proves SDUI can handle dynamic list rendering, not just static forms

Deliverable: Storybook story showing Meus Vouchers rendered via SDUI engine.
Side-by-side comparison with the real screen (SP_0004.6) if available.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0005.1 — SDUI Demo: Extrato (Transaction History) as SDUI
- [ ] Create an SDUI JSON payload to render the Extrato screen via ScreenRenderer.

Context:
- Figma reference: .claude/tiles/figma-018.png
- Needs new SDUI adapter types:
  - points_card: balance display with "Pontos disponíveis" + "Pontos a liberar"
  - tab_bar: horizontal tab navigation (Todos / A liberar / A expirar)
  - transaction_item: row with icon, description, points value, date grouping
  - date_separator: section header with date label
- This is the most complex screen — proves SDUI can handle tabbed layouts
  and grouped lists
- Story should show: populated list with date groups, tab switching, empty tab

Deliverable: Storybook story showing Extrato rendered via SDUI engine.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0005.2 — SDUI Demo: Onboarding Carousel as SDUI

      via ScreenRenderer.

Context:

- The onboarding shows a carousel of slides:
  - "Use pontos para pagar menos na fatura"
  - "Use pontos para criar um voucher"
  - "O voucher vale para mais de uma vez"
  - "O desconto é aplicado automaticamente"
- Needs new SDUI adapter types:
  - carousel: container with swipeable children and dot indicators
  - illustration: renders an image/SVG illustration centered on screen
- Story should show: all carousel slides navigable via controls

Deliverable: Storybook story showing onboarding carousel rendered via SDUI.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0005.3 — SDUI Comparison Dashboard Story

      → Current implementation → SDUI version, for each screen.

Context:
- This is the culminating story that makes the case for SDUI adoption
- Layout: 3 columns per row (Figma screenshot | Real screen | SDUI render)
- Rows: one per screen (voucher form, summary, conclusion, rules, list, etc.)
- Figma screenshots: load from .claude/tiles/figma-*.png as static images
- Real screens: reference SP_0004.x stories
- SDUI renders: reference SP_0002.5 + SP_0005.x stories
- Add annotations highlighting differences and SDUI advantages:
  - "Same renderer, different JSON → different product variant"
  - "No code change needed to update text/layout"
  - "Content team can edit JSON without developer involvement"

Deliverable: A "SDUI Comparison" story page showing the evolution from
Figma → Code → SDUI for all voucher screens.

- [DONE] [P0] remove manual mocks importing for superApp imports ('~/*') in
storybook stories and add a custom alias resolver in storybook/vite config.
  > tildeResolverPlugin already existed in .storybook/main.ts (lines 188-202)
  >   resolving ~/store → mocks/store.ts, ~/redux/store/storage → mocks/redux-store-storage.ts,
  >   and other ~/X paths → src/X.
  > Root cause of PointsScreen story failure: circular dependency in slices.ts
  >   importing from ../index (barrel) which re-exports services that import ~/store.
  > Fix: changed src/services/store/slices.ts to import directly from individual
  >   ./[name].store files instead of ../index barrel.
  > Verified: PointsScreen story renders correctly; SDUI stories unaffected.
  > Screenshot: .storybook/screenshots/points-screen-loaded.png

# SP989887GV
- [DONE] [P1] Some resources described in rfc-conteudo-dinamico.txt are not
implemented nor have tasks in progress.txt, review it and add new tasks.
  > Reviewed RFC sections 3.1–3.5 against current implementation.
  > Found 5 gaps: JSON Logic evaluator, validation executor, rich text adapter,
  >   component visibility (visible conditional), button enabled conditional.
  > Created tasks: SP_0006.0 (JSON Logic), SP_0006.1 (visibility + enabled),
  >   SP_0006.2 (validation), SP_0006.3 (rich text adapter).

Context: we identified that JSON-logic and another things was not considered,
fix it.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

================================================================================
=== PHASE 6: RFC MISSING IMPLEMENTATIONS ======================================
================================================================================

NOTE: These tasks implement RFC features that have types defined (SP_0002.0)
but no runtime code. They complete the SDUI engine to full RFC spec.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.0 — JSON Logic Evaluator
- [ ] Implement the JSON Logic rule evaluator described in RFC section 3.5.

Context:
- RFC defines operators: var (data access), ==, !=, >, <, >=, <= (comparison),
  and, or, ! (logical)
- Types already exist: SDUIRule, SDUIComparisonRule, SDUILogicalRule,
  SDUIVarOperator, SDUIConditional (in src/services/sdui/models/index.ts)
- Needs a function: evaluateRule(rule: SDUIRule, context: SDUIContext): boolean
- var operator uses resolveContextPath from interpolation.ts
- Invalid/malformed rules should return false (RFC 6.4: "Regras inválidas
  retornam false")
- For `visible` conditionals specifically: invalid rules should default to
  true (show component) — hiding content by default on bad data is worse UX
- Must be pure function, no side effects
- Reference: https://jsonlogic.com/

Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/jsonLogic.ts with evaluateRule() +
comprehensive unit tests covering all operators and edge cases.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.1 — Component Visibility & Button Enabled Conditionals
- [ ] Wire the JSON Logic evaluator into ScreenRenderer for component
      visibility and button enabled state.

Context:
- SDUIComponent already has optional `visible: SDUIConditional` field
- ScreenRenderer currently ignores `visible` — should skip rendering when
  evaluateRule returns false
- ButtonAdapter should process `enabled: { rule: SDUIRule }` in props —
  disable button when rule evaluates to false
- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
- Update existing ScreenRenderer.test.tsx and adapters.test.tsx
- Add Storybook demo showing conditional visibility toggle

Deliverable: Components with `visible` conditionals are hidden/shown based on
context. Buttons with `enabled` rules are disabled/enabled dynamically.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.2 — Input Validation Rules
- [ ] Implement validation rule execution for SDUI input components.

Context:
- RFC section 3.5 defines validation pattern:
  { "validation": { "rules": [{ "rule": {...}, "message": "error text" }] } }
- Types exist: SDUIValidationRule, SDUIValidation
- PointsInputAdapter has textError prop but no validation integration
- Needs: validateField(value, validation: SDUIValidation, context): string | null
  (returns first failing rule's message, or null if valid)
- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
- Should integrate with PointsInputAdapter's error display
- Scope: validation utility function only. Form-level orchestration (when to
  trigger: blur/change/submit) is a separate concern for action handlers
- Add Storybook demo showing validation in action

Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/validation.ts with validateField() +
integration into PointsInputAdapter + unit tests.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.3 — Rich Text Adapter
- [ ] Implement the rich_text component adapter described in RFC section 3.3.

Context:
- RFC defines rich text as array of typed nodes:
  - text: { type: "text", value: string, bold?: boolean, italic?: boolean, color?: string }
  - link: { type: "link", text: string, to: string, trackEvent?: string }
  - icon: { type: "icon", name: string, color?: string, size?: number }
  - linebreak: { type: "linebreak" }
- Types already defined: SDUIRichText, SDUITextNode, SDUILinkNode, SDUIIconNode,
  SDUILineBreakNode (in models/index.ts)
- Should render inline — all nodes in a single wrapping Text component
  (React Native supports nested Text for inline formatting)
- Links should use DS Text with underline style (similar to LinkAdapter)
- Icons use DS Icon component inline
- Linebreak nodes render as `\n` inside Text (not DOM br element —
  React Native Text supports newline characters)
- Register as "rich_text" type in adapter registry
- Add mock payload using rich_text to test inline formatting

Deliverable: RichTextAdapter registered in SDUI registry + unit tests +
Storybook demo showing mixed bold/link/icon inline text.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


# SP008767GX
- [ ] Find all design system component used in our screens and map all of it to
the SDUI system, including stories.

Context: ...

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP9999999999 [P99] Multiple tasks
- [ ] ensure stories are handled by `npm run lint`
- [ ] run `npm run lint` and register new tasks for fixing everything
- [ ] implement the task described in ./TASK.pdf
     - Prepare the frontend
     - Test every screen scenario in storybook
     - Create plan/spec to the backend to implement
- [ ] Read all screens and plan what frontend action handlers we will need to
implement from the frontend side (like link redirections, validations, sum
values, custom logics, etc.) in order to make all screen possible to be recreated
 using the SDUI engine then plan the architecture to handle it then map the
 handlers.


--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SPMAIS32272X0001 Merge branch feature/SPMAIS-32272

- [ ] Plan and add tasks for integrate pages from SPMAIS-32271 to storybook,
including API connection (see files inside ./.claude/resources)
- [ ] implement task described in ./.claude/resources/SPMAIS-34887.pdf

- [DONE] make JSON Editor a Tool open by default on the right side
  > Created .storybook/manager.ts with addons.setConfig():
  >   panelPosition: 'right', rightPanelWidth: 400,
  >   selectedPanel: 'sdui-json-editor/panel'.
  > JSON Editor panel now opens on the right side by default with 400px width.
  > Screenshot validated: json-editor-right-panel.png.
